## 1 Introduction

The escalating global trade in counterfeit products has established anti-counterfeiting technologies as critical safeguards for protecting corporate interests and maintaining market order. Quick Response (QR) codes have gained significant traction in product authentication and traceability systems due to their superior data encoding capacity and broad device compatibility. However, conventional QR codes face substantial security vulnerabilities to mass replication and forgery, primarily attributable to their open encoding standards and standardized graphical structures (Guo et al. [2024](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#ref-CR8 "Guo Z, Wang S, Zheng Z et al (2024) Printer source identification of quick response codes using residual attention network and smartphones. Eng Appl Artif Intell 131:107822"); Wu et al. [2024](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#ref-CR15 "Wu R, Liu N, Peng G et al (2024) An innovative method for securing QR codes against counterfeits in supply chain management. In: 2024 IEEE 14th Annual Computing and Communication Workshop and Conference (CCWC), pp 0589–0596")). In response to these security limitations, both academic and industrial communities have developed various enhancement strategies. Current technological approaches predominantly focus on incorporating non-replicable physical or digital features, which can be categorized into four principal implementation pathways: (1) Physically unclonable functions (PUFs) and micro-features: Yan et al. ( [2020](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#ref-CR21 "Yan Y, Zou Z, Xie H et al (2020) An IoT-based anti-counterfeiting system using visual features on QR code. IEEE Internet Things J 8(8):6789–6799")) integrate visual features with QR codes, using natural textures and printed micro-features for authentication. Zheng et al. ( [2021](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#ref-CR25 "Zheng Z, Zheng H, Ju J et al (2021) A system for identifying an anti-counterfeiting pattern based on the statistical difference in key image regions. Expert Syst Appl 183:115410")) combine a standard 2D barcode with a fine-textured pattern made of binary pixels. (2) Watermarks: Chen et al. ( [2023](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#ref-CR4 "Chen J, Dong L, Wang R et al (2023) Physical anti-copying semi-robust random watermarking for QR code. In: Zhao X, Tang Z, Comesaña-Alfaro P, Piva A (eds) Digital forensics and watermarking. Lecture Notes in Computer Science, Springer, Cham, pp 131–146")) describe a physical anti-copying technique for QR codes based on semi-robust randomized watermarking. Pan et al. ( [2021b](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#ref-CR12 "Pan J-S, Sun X-X, Chu S-C et al (2021b) Digital watermarking with improved SMS applied for QR code. Eng Appl Artif Intell 97:104049–104061")) improve the robustness and reliability of watermarking by enhancing the spatial distribution of embedded features. (3) Copy detection patterns (CDPs): Chaban et al. ( [2022](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#ref-CR3 "Chaban R, Taran O, Tutt J et al (2022) Printing variability of copy detection patterns. In: 2022 IEEE International Workshop on Information Forensics and Security (WIFS), pp 1–6")) investigate the print variability of replica detection patterns and propose an analysis method to improve their detection accuracy. Tkachenko et al. ( [2016](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#ref-CR14 "Tkachenko I, Puech W, Destruel C et al (2016) Two-level QR code for private message sharing and document authentication. IEEE Trans Inf Forensics Secur 11(3):571–583")) and Xie and Tan ( [2021](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#ref-CR19 "Xie S, Tan H-Z (2021) An anti-counterfeiting architecture for traceability system based on modified two-level quick response codes. Electronics 10(3):320")) introduce a two-level QR code consisting of a public layer and a private textured layer. Present a copy-protection scheme based on spectral and spatial barcode channel models, embedding markers that change distinctly during scanning and printing to differentiate originals from copies. (4) Halftones: Xie et al. ( [2021](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#ref-CR20 "Xie N, Zhang Q, Chen Y et al (2021) Low-cost anti-copying 2D barcode by exploiting channel noise characteristics. IEEE Trans Multimed 23:3752–3767"), [2022](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#ref-CR18 "Xie N, Chen J, Chen Y et al (2022) Detection of information hiding at anti-copying 2D barcodes. IEEE Trans Circ Syst Video Technol 32(1):437–450")) introduce double authentication by embedding information into halftone dot placement, reducing spacing distances via high-order modulation to amplify differences after printing and scanning.

**Table 1 Comprehensive comparison of anti-counterfeiting QR code innovations and their authentication algorithms**

[Full size table](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0/tables/1)

Anti-counterfeiting QR codes establish verifiable digital fingerprints for authentication systems through their structured features, while authentication algorithms complete the genuineness verification by extracting and comparing key information embedded in predefined characteristics. Together, these two components form a complete self-verifying closed-loop system. The core of authentication algorithms constitutes a classification task fundamentally reliant on feature extraction, with prevailing methodologies falling into three principal paradigms: (1) Machine learning texture-based methods: RALBGC (Khadiri et al. [2018](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#ref-CR10 "Khadiri I, Kas M, Merabet Y et al (2018) Repulsive-and attractive local binary gradient contours: new and efficient feature descriptors for texture classification. Inf Sci 467:634–653")) improves noise resilience through preprocessing and adaptive scale selection. LGONBP (Song et al. [2021](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#ref-CR13 "Song T, Feng J, Luo L et al (2021) Robust texture description using local grouped order pattern and non-local binary pattern. IEEE Trans Circ Syst Video Technol 31(1):189–202")) integrates local structural modeling with non-local pattern comparison, capturing both fine-grained details and global layout characteristics. For color textures, Dong et al. ( [2021](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#ref-CR5 "Dong Y, Jin M, Li X et al (2021) Compact interchannel sampling difference descriptor for color texture classification. IEEE Trans Circ Syst Video Technol 31(5):1684–1696")) exploit inter-channel variations to construct compact yet informative descriptors. RAMBP (Alpaslan [2022](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#ref-CR2 "Alpaslan N (2022) Neutrosophic set based local binary pattern for texture classification. Expert Syst Appl 209:118350")) introduces neutral sets to model uncertainty in texture patterns, offering a new perspective on handling ambiguous regions. Pan et al. ( [2021a](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#ref-CR11 "Pan Z, Hu S, Wu X et al (2021a) Adaptive center pixel selection strategy in local binary pattern for texture classification. Expert Syst Appl 180:115123")) enhance computational efficiency by adaptively selecting central pixels for encoding. (2) Deep learning methods: ResNet18 (He et al. [2016](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#ref-CR9 "He K, Zhang X, Ren S et al (2016) Deep residual learning for image recognition. In: 2016 IEEE conference on computer vision and pattern recognition. pp 770–778")), built upon residual connections, alleviates training difficulties in deep networks and serves as a foundational architecture across vision tasks. FG-DPANet (You et al. [2023](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#ref-CR23 "You C, Zheng H, Guo Z et al (2023) Identification of a printed anti-counterfeiting code based on feature guidance double pool attention networks. Comput Mater Contin 75(2):3431–3452")) improves recognition accuracy for security-related patterns by combining multi-scale representation with attention mechanisms that highlight salient regions. Yang et al. ( [2022](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#ref-CR22 "Yang J, Xiao S, Li A et al (2022) MSTA-Net: forgery detection by generating manipulation trace based on multi-scale self-texture attention. IEEE Trans Circ Syst Video Technol 32(7):4854–4866")) detect image forgeries by constructing multi-scale self-texture attention graphs, effectively identifying inconsistencies in structural regularity. Florindo et al. ( [2021](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#ref-CR7 "Florindo JB, Lee Y-S, Jun K et al (2021) VisGraphNet: a complex network interpretation of convolutional neural features. Inf Sci 543:296–308")) improve the interpretability of CNNs in texture analysis by mapping learned features into complex network representations. In follow-up work, Florindo and Laureano ( [2023](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#ref-CR6 "Florindo JB, Laureano EE (2023) BoFF: a bag of fuzzy deep features for texture recognition. Expert Syst Appl 219:119627")) propose fuzzy-based feature bundling to capture uncertainty in texture responses, achieving strong performance with enhanced robustness. (3) Template-matching methods: Wang et al. ( [2023](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#ref-CR17 "Wang T, Zheng H, You C et al (2023) A texture-hidden anti-counterfeiting QR code and authentication method. Sensors 23(2):795")) present DFDA, which combines spectral analysis with QR code decoding principles to verify authenticity and assess image quality. Subsequently, Wang et al. ( [2024](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#ref-CR16 "Wang T, Zheng H, Guo Z et al (2024) Anti-counterfeiting textured pattern. Vis Comput 40(3):2139–2160")) propose TACA, a cascaded framework that jointly models structural interpretability, frequency-domain characteristics, and local keypoint matching to achieve robust verification under complex distortions. Zheng et al. ( [2022](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#ref-CR24 "Zheng Z, Xu B, Ju J et al (2022) Circumferential local ternary pattern: new and efficient feature descriptors for anti-counterfeiting pattern identification. IEEE Trans Inf Forensics Secur 17:970–981")) introduce CLTP, a ring-based local ternary pattern that partitions images into concentric regions to extract spatially organized texture features for tamper detection.

Table [1](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#Tab1) systematically summarizes representative anti-counterfeiting QR code innovations and their authentication algorithms. Comprehensive analysis reveals that while existing research has developed multiple technological pathways to enhance QR code security and achieved basic anti-counterfeiting functionality while maintaining readability and robustness, three fundamental challenges persist: First, the Balance Challenge: Current solutions struggle to achieve an optimal balance between security strength and practicality. High-security designs typically require specialized materials or complex processes, significantly increasing costs, while cost-effective alternatives prove inadequate against sophisticated forgery attacks. Second, the Adaptability Challenge: Authentication algorithms exhibit insufficient generalization capability in complex real-world scenarios. When confronted with various practical interference factors (such as noise, blur, illumination variations, and scratches), most existing methods experience substantial performance degradation, limiting their reliable deployment across diverse application environments. Third, the Architectural Challenge: There is a lack of co-design of interpretability and lightweight optimization at the system level. Most current authentication frameworks fail to effectively integrate macroscopic structural information with microscopic texture features, which not only restricts further improvement in recognition accuracy but also hinders meeting the dual requirements of algorithmic transparency and computational efficiency in practical applications. Despite continuous advancements in anti-counterfeiting technologies, these bottlenecks continue to constrain large-scale commercial implementation. To address these challenges, this study proposes an innovative anti-counterfeiting QR code solution that achieves both high security and excellent practical performance through the synergistic integration of a physical feature generation mechanism and a dual-domain authentication framework. The method employs a fusion strategy combining multispectral color matrices with adaptive grayscale gradient technology, transforming inherent micro-mechanical defects and toner diffusion phenomena during printing into highly randomized anti-counterfeiting features. This enables the generation of secure textures with physically unclonable characteristics under conventional printing conditions. The proposed scheme maintains complete standard QR code readability even at minimal dimensions (1.02 \\times 1.02 cm) while remaining fully compatible with commercial printers and ordinary paper, significantly reducing deployment costs for high-security anti-counterfeiting solutions.

For the authentication mechanism, we developed a light-weight, interpretable verification system based on wavelet-Fourier dual-domain feature fusion that achieves high-precision authentication without relying on black-box models. This scheme is rooted in the orthogonal decomposition properties of the two transforms at the mathematical level and builds a more complete signal representation system by synergizing their unique advantages. Specifically, wavelet decomposition generates basis functions suitable for multi-scale features through a multi-resolution analysis mechanism, which can accurately capture singular points and transient features in the signal. Its continuous wavelet transform form achieves flexible dynamic representation of the signal through the scaling and translation operations of the mother wavelet. In contrast, the short-time Fourier transform uses complex sine waves as basis functions, ensuring the best frequency localization while fully retaining the key phase information for characterizing periodic structures and texture patterns. This orthogonality at the mathematical level enables the joint representation to break through the performance boundaries of single-spectrum analysis methods and achieve comprehensive extraction of signal features. At the system architecture design level, this framework systematically applies the above complementary characteristics through a hierarchical decision-making mechanism. In the primary processing stage, a two-level discrete wavelet transform is implemented to efficiently capture the basic structural information of the image by extracting the approximation coefficients and effectively suppress noise interference. The feature extraction process simultaneously considers the intra-channel coefficients and inter-channel difference features to construct a composite feature set. A similarity assessment based on the correlation coefficient generates a probabilistic authentication index to quantify the morphological matching degree between the test code and the reference template. When the judgment result falls into the decision-making fuzzy zone, the system automatically activates the Fourier domain analysis module for indepth verification. At this stage, a carefully designed frequency direction vector is used to extract phase information with fuzzy invariance. This phase information is quantized locally to generate binary codes and then aggregated into a high-dimensional statistical histogram to represent local frequency characteristics. The final classifier uses a distance metric method to evaluate the similarity of feature distributions and effectively distinguish between genuine and counterfeiting codes. This dual-spectral fusion method demonstrates multi-dimensional theoretical advantages: Firstly, it accurately captures the singular points and key phase information in the signal, significantly compensating for the feature blind spots of single-spectrum analysis methods. Secondly, the hierarchical architecture intelligently allocates computing resources. It only activates the complex Fourier analysis when the discriminative power of the wavelet domain features is insufficient, thus balancing efficiency and accuracy. Thirdly, the feature space significantly enhances the inter- class separability by projecting to high dimensions, maximizing the inter-class distance between genuine and counterfeiting codes. Fourthly, it has robustness against mul- tiple attack modes at the theoretical level. Wavelet features are naturally resistant to geometric transformations, while Fourier features remain stable under photometric changes.

To comprehensively evaluate system performance, we constructed an integrated dataset covering six typical attack scenarios: scanning-printing, reprinting, noise, blur, illumination, and scratches. Experimental results demonstrate that our solution significantly outperforms existing mainstream technologies in resistance to reprinting, interference immunity, and environmental adaptability while maintaining high readability and low deployment costs. The system achieves stable authentication without requiring training processes, providing a viable technical pathway for high-security identification applications in resource-constrained environments. The main contributions of this paper can be summarized as follows:

- The development of an anti-counterfeiting QR code generation mechanism based on printing randomness transformation, overcoming the traditional trade-off between security and usability;

- The design of an interpretable dual-spectral authentication framework that achieves high-precision, lightweight authentication through wavelet-Fourier domain feature collaboration;

- The establishment of a comprehensive anti-counterfeiting evaluation benchmark that provides a standardized foundation for algorithm comparison and technological advan-cement in the field.


The rest of the paper is organized as follows: Section [2](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#Sec2) describes the printing mechanism. Section [3](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#Sec3) presents the design of color QR codes. Section [4](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#Sec4) explains the dual-spectral feature authentication method. Section [5](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#Sec9) presents and evaluates the experimental results. Finally, in Section [6](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#Sec16), conclusions are drawn from this study along with suggestions for future research directions.

## 2 Analysis of printing mechanism

This paper investigates the authentication process of printed codes and shows the generating process of genuine and counterfeiting CACQR codes in Fig. [1](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#Fig1). In this process, the authorized party generates digital codes and prints them out using an officially authorized printer, and then pastes them on the goods or documents to be circulated. If counterfeiters do not have access to the original digital images, they can only scan the codes on the merchandises and print them after a series of image processing techniques. However, counterfeiters can easily produce reprint codes if they have access to the original digital images. Ultimately, to verify the authenticity of the codes, consumers can use their smartphones to capture patterns and compare them to templates captured by a scanner from authentic products that have been printed. By comparing the two patterns, consumers can effectively determine whether or not the anti-counterfeiting code is authentic.

**Fig. 1**

![Fig. 1](https://media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1007%2Fs44443-025-00453-0/MediaObjects/44443_2025_453_Fig1_HTML.png)

[Full size image](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0/figures/1)

The generating process of genuine and counterfeiting CACQR codes

The existence of differences between genuine and counterfeit products is mainly influenced by the following four factors. Firstly, differences in printers contribute to variations in print results, as different printing technologies (such as laser or inkjet) and paper materials can affect color reproduction, detail sharpness, and color saturation. Secondly, human factors such as printer settings, maintenance, operator skill, and environmental conditions (such as temperature, humidity, and lighting) can also impact print results. Additionally, variations in source files can result differences in print. Even the same pattern, when opened or processed on different computers and software, may differ in print results due to differences in software versions, processing algorithms, compression, and other factors that affect the color reproduction and detail performance. Finally, errors and distortions in the conversion of digital signals can cause variations in printed results. During the printing process, the digital signal must to be converted to an analog signal that can be processed by the printer.

The same digital pattern can still be distinguished when scanned and printed due to the use of different color halftone algorithms. The color halftone algorithm is responsible for converting a continuous color image into a finite color image by controlling the size, density, and distribution of the color dots. This technique aims to visually provide rich color variability and detailed representations. Counterfeiters face difficulties in producing forgeries, as minor differences in pitch patterns can be amplified or altered, resulting in noticeable differences between the counterfeit and genuine products. Color halftone algorithms can improve the security of QR codes and reduce the possibility of forgery. However, it is crucial to optimize these algorithms when applying them to avoid reducing color information details and causing blurring of QR codes, which may negatively affect the decoding process. Furthermore, the use of color halftone algorithms may impact the anti-jamming capability of QR codes. By optimizing algorithms and parameters, QR codes can be accurately read in various environments with improved anti-jamming ability. The effect of ambient lighting, noise, and other factors on QR codes can be reduced. When choosing a color halftone algorithm, it is crucial to consider both the speed of encoding and decoding. Some algorithms may slow down the encoding and decoding process, which can impact productivity. Therefore, it is essential to thoroughly evaluate and select the most appropriate color halftone algorithm. Factors to consider include anti-counterfeiting measures, readability, anti-jamming ability, and processing speed. These considerations ensure that QR codes meet specific application requirements.

Several common color halftone algorithms and their impact on the performance of QR codes will be discussed below. One of these algorithms is amplitude modulation screening (AMS), which represents different color shades by changing the size of the dots. In anti-counterfeiting QR codes, additional information can be embedded by adjusting the dot size. However, this information is nearly imperceptible to the naked eye and requires specific scanning equipment to read. Frequency modulation screening (FMS) is a technique that uses smaller, denser dots to indicate color shades by varying the density of the dots. This technique improves the robustness of QR codes, especially when the print is blurred or damaged. Error diffusion (ED) achieves a halftone effect by comparing each pixel in an image with the surrounding pixels and spreading the difference into neighboring pixels. While this method can improve image quality, it may also decrease the readability of QR codes. Blue noise halftone (BNH) reduces the moire phenomenon during printing while maintaining image detail, based on the human eye’s sensitivity to different frequencies. It is very favorable for anti-counterfeiting QR codes as it improves the readability of QR codes and the concealment of the anti-counterfeiting features. Dot watermarking (DW) is another effective method that uses dot features in halftone images to obscure watermarking information. This provides an additional layer of security to QR codes without compromising their functionality.

[Full size image](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0/figures/2)

The design process of the CACQR code

Reprinting of the same digital pattern leads to microscopic differences due to the random diffusion of toner or ink. This randomness in color distribution and texture detail features at the microscopic level makes it challenging for counterfeiters to achieve a similar effect through reprinting, thus allowing the differentiation between authenticity and forgery. The official printer used in this paper employs the CMYK four-color halftone algorithm to produce color printing effects. The CMYK mode combines cyan, magenta, yellow, and black toners for multi-color printing effects. The basic principles of the algorithm are as follows: 1) Convert the RGB mode image to CMYK mode using a color-space conversion algorithm for subsequent processing. 2) Split the CMYK image into four separate channels, representing the color information of cyan, magenta, yellow, and black. 3) Apply a halftone algorithm on each channel to convert the continuous color information into black-and-white or limited color images. 4) Remerge the halftone processed CMYK channels to produce the final color halftone image.

## 3 Design process of the CACQR code

To ensure anti-counterfeiting of goods, a one-item, one-code design scheme is adopted. Each item has a unique anti-counterfeiting code, where the semantics of the item is the index of the pattern. An anti-counterfeiting code is a unique identifier used to verify the authenticity of an item. Each item is typically assigned a unique security code and attached with a QR code to ensure traceability and authentication. Consumers can use a scanning device, such as a smartphone, to read the QR code on an item. Once scanned, the device sends the captured code to a specialized verification platform for authentication. The platform matches the product number with the corresponding code template and compares it to the code provided by the consumer. If the codes match, the product is deemed authentic. If they do not match, the product may be counterfeit. This process ensures fast and reliable verification of the authenticity of goods. Figure [2](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#Fig2) illustrates the design process of the code. The design of the code involves five steps to ensure its uniqueness and anti-counterfeiting capability. These steps also effectively protect the security of the goods, as will be described below.

Step 1: Generate QR code

A QR code with a specified version and error correction level is generated, denoted as F\_a(x, y) \\in \\mathbb {R}^{N \\times N \\times 3}, where _N_ represents the spatial dimension.

Step 2: Scale transformation based on physical printing parameters

**Fig. 3**

![Fig. 3](https://media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1007%2Fs44443-025-00453-0/MediaObjects/44443_2025_453_Fig3_HTML.png)

[Full size image](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0/figures/3)

The Anti-counterfeiting system flow of the CACQR code

To adapt the QR code to the target physical size and printer resolution, a scale factor _S_ is introduced. Let DPI (dots per inch) denote the printer resolution and UNIT (inches) denote the desired physical size of the code. The scaling factor is computed as:

\\begin{aligned} S = \\frac{\\text {DPI} \\times \\text {UNIT}}{N} \\end{aligned}

(1)


The scaled QR code, denoted as F\_b(x', y') \\in \\mathbb {R}^{(N \\cdot S) \\times (N \\cdot S) \\times 3}, is obtained by replicating each original pixel over an S \\times S block. Formally, for each color channel C \\in \\{R, G, B\\}:

\\begin{aligned} F\_b^C(x', y') = {\\left\\{ \\begin{array}{ll} 0, & \\text {if } F\_a^C(x, y) = 0 \\\ 255, & \\text {if } F\_a^C(x, y) = 255 \\end{array}\\right. } \\end{aligned}

(2)


here, the scaled coordinates are defined over the intervals:

\\begin{aligned} x' \\in \\left\[ (x \\cdot S) - (S - 1),\ x \\cdot S \\right\] , \\quad y' \\in \\left\[ (y \\cdot S) - (S - 1),\ y \\cdot S \\right\] \\end{aligned}

(3)


Step 3: Multi-level grayscale assignment via stochastic sampling

To mitigate the risk of counterfeiting, stochastic multi-level grayscale values are assigned to the black regions. Let \[\\mu \_{\\text {min}}^C, \\mu \_{\\text {max}}^C\] \\subseteq \[0, 255\] denote the grayscale range for channel _C_, where \\mu \_{\\text {min}}^C \\le \\mu \_{\\text {max}}^C. The updated code F\_c^C(x', y') is given by:

\\begin{aligned} F\_c^C(x', y') = {\\left\\{ \\begin{array}{ll} \\text {rand}\\left( \[\\mu \_{\\text {min}}^C, \\mu \_{\\text {max}}^C\]\\right) , & \\text {if } F\_b^C(x', y') = 0 \\\ 255, & \\text {if } F\_b^C(x', y') = 255 \\end{array}\\right. } \\end{aligned}

(4)


here, \\text {rand}(\[a, b\]) denotes a uniformly random integer in \[ _a_, _b_\].

Step 4: Multi-channel fusion

The final color pattern is formed by combining the three channels:

\\begin{aligned} F\_c(x', y') = \\left( F\_c^R(x', y'),\ F\_c^G(x', y'),\ F\_c^B(x', y') \\right) \\end{aligned}

(5)


Step 5: Localization border addition

A border of width \\text {Edge} \\in \\mathbb {Z}^+ is added. The final anti-counterfeiting code has dimensions:

\\begin{aligned} (W + 2 \\cdot \\text {Edge}) \\times (H + 2 \\cdot \\text {Edge}) \\times 3 \\end{aligned}

(6)


here, W = H = N \\cdot S. The pixel values are:

\\begin{aligned} F\_{\\text {final}}(x'', y'') = {\\left\\{ \\begin{array}{ll} F\_c(x', y'), & x'' = \\text {Edge} + x',\ y'' = \\text {Edge} + y' \\\ 0, & \\text {otherwise} \\end{array}\\right. } \\end{aligned}

(7)


Anti-counterfeiting QR codes utilize multi-spectral color matrices and dynamic grayscale gradients to concurrently enhance cryptographic security and aesthetic appeal. This chromatic complexity presents significant spectral duplication challenges for counterfeiters, thereby substantially increasing the difficulty of pattern replication compared to monochromatic designs.

## 4 The proposed authentication method

### 4.1 Authentication flow

As shown in Fig. [3](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#Fig3), the proposed anti-counterfeiting system consists of three core components: generation and capture of anti-counterfeiting codes, cloud-based data storage, analysis, and verification, and a smartphone application for end users. Together, these components form a complete technical workflow—from physical label production to real-time terminal authentication. First, in the code generation and capture phase, unique digital images embedded with distinctive texture patterns are generated in bulk using a dedicated design platform and subsequently printed into physical labels via high-precision printing devices. High-resolution industrial cameras are then employed to capture standardized images of the printed codes under controlled conditions, ensuring consistent image quality. The resulting high-fidelity images serve as reference samples and are uploaded to the cloud platform for centralized management. Second, the cloud platform enables secure storage and intelligent processing of authentication data. The collected reference images are used to construct an authoritative feature database, which supports the training and optimization of counterfeit detection models. When a user initiates a verification request, the captured query image is transmitted to the cloud server, where it undergoes preprocessing operations to enhance robustness against imaging distortions. A pre-trained classification model then extracts multi-dimensional features from the processed image and compares them with registered templates in the database. The similarity score is computed to determine whether the sample is genuine or counterfeit. Finally, end users perform interactive authentication through a mobile application. By simply scanning the target code with their smartphone camera, the image is automatically uploaded to initiate the cloud-based verification process. The authentication result is returned to the app, parsed, and presented in an intuitive format, enabling fast and user-friendly identity validation.

The authentication flow of the anti-counterfeiting code shown in Fig. [4](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#Fig4) consists of quality assessment and dual-spectral feature authentication. The captured code is first detected to extract the anti-counterfeiting region. Then, a quality assessment scheme is used to test the quality of the anti-counterfeiting code, ensuring the reliability of the authentication process. If the quality does not meet the requirements, a recapture request is sent to the user. Finally, the dual-spectral feature authentication is divided into two steps. In the first step, wavelet-domain feature are extracted from the input image and a probability measure _p_ (where p \\in \[0,1\]) is calculated, which indicates the likelihood that the input image is genuine. For samples with p \\ge p2 or p \\le p1 , an authentication decision can be made in the first stage, while when an authentication decision can be made in the first stage, while when p1< p < p2 , the process moves to the second stage for further evaluation. In the second stage, feature is extracted from the Fourier-domain and the final authentication decision is made using a classifier. In the anti-counterfeiting code database experiments, the values of p1 and p2 are used to control the decision boundary of the classifier. The gap between genuine and fake products is widened as much as possible while ensuring completely correct classification.

Anti-counterfeiting code enhancement and quality assessment.

During the cycle, the anti-counterfeiting code may be battered, and the image quality may suffer from defocus blur and motion blur caused by the complexity of the environment and the use of handheld cameras. To ensure the reliability and accuracy of the code, it is necessary to enhance the image quality and evaluate the quality of the obtained. Only qualified anti-counterfeiting codes can be authenticated. In this work, the brightness and contrast of the images are adjusted to improve the image and thus make the details clearer. This paper achieves the enhancement and quality assessment of anti-counterfeiting code images through four core steps: channel decomposition, adaptive contrast enhancement, energy gradient calculation, and quality decision-making. Regarding the specific process, please refer to Algorithm 1. Typically, high-quality images with clear details and sharp edges have higher values, while low-quality images with issues such as blur and distortion have lower values. Determining reasonable values for images under complex interference requires numerous experiments.

### 4.2 Dual-spectral feature

Figure [5](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#Fig5) shows the visualization of genuine and counterfeit codes after image enhancement and passed quality assessment. By enlarging the images, traces of illegal copying become more evident. However, developing an effective texture classifier to recognize the code is challenging and requires prior knowledge, and the paper proposes a dual-spectral feature for this purpose.

**Fig. 6**

![Fig. 6](https://media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1007%2Fs44443-025-00453-0/MediaObjects/44443_2025_453_Fig6_HTML.png)

[Full size image](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0/figures/6)

The extraction process of Wavelet-domain feature. (1) Extracting channels in RGB color space. (2) Capturing texture features in each channel, we propose two descriptors: intra-channel feature (shown in ( **a**)) and inter-channel difference feature (shown in ( **b**)). (3) Encoding vector features

#### 4.2.1 Wavelet-domain feature

Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT) is a method of decomposing a signal into different frequency sub-bands, which can decompose the original image into multiple approximation coefficients and detail coefficients with the ability of multi-scale analysis and multi-directional analysis. In particular, the approximation coefficients contain the global information of the image, while the detail coefficients contain the detailed information of the image. For the tested anti-counterfeiting pattern, obtained after preprocessing, after wavelet decomposition of it at the scale of _L_, we can get approximation coefficients G\_0 and 3\*L detail coefficients {\\{ {G\_{ij}}\\} \_{i = 1,2,3,...,L,j = 1,2,3}}.

The wavelet-domain approximation coefficients are selected for feature extraction due to the following advantages: 1) Multi-scale invariance, which ensures robustness against rotation, scaling, and distortion transformations. 2) Information integrity, preserving critical shape, texture, luminance, and chromatic characteristics necessary for recognition. 3) Computational efficiency, as it reduces dimensionality while maintaining high processing speed and storage economy. While DWT can theoretically be factorized indefinitely, in practice it can be optimized to extract appropriate signal features and achieve desired compression ratios depending on the requirements. It is worth noting that as the decomposition level increases, the resolution of each band decreases, which may affect the ability to represent the signal features, but results in additional band terms and lower entropy values, thus achieving a higher compression ratio. Therefore, the DWT decomposition must be optimized on a case-by-case basis to extract appropriate features and achieve the desired compression.

In color texture images, different channels are not independent, and there are relationships between them. To avoid losing color texture information by modelling three color channels separately, this paper models the intra-channel features and inter-channel features separately. Figure [6](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#Fig6) illustrates the color feature extraction process under a two-level DWT decomposition.

The proposed authentication framework utilizes a multi-stage pipeline that integrates wavelet-based feature extraction with similarity assessment. Initially, for an input image E^C\\in \\mathbb {R}^{H\\times W\\times 3}, each color channel C\\in \\{R, G, B\\} undergoes a two-level discrete wavelet transform. The first-level decomposition of channel C produces four subband coefficient sets:

\\begin{aligned} \\mathcal {D^C\_{\\textrm{1}}}= {LL^C\_1, LH^C\_1, HL^C\_1, HH^C\_1} \\end{aligned}

(8)


here, LL^C\_1 contains approximation coefficients and LH^C\_1, HL^C\_1, HH^C\_1 represent detail coefficients.

Next, second-level decomposition is applied to LL^C\_1, producing:

\\begin{aligned} \\mathcal {D^C\_{\\textrm{2}}} = {LL^C\_2, LH^C\_2, HL^C\_2, HH^C\_2} \\cup {LH^C\_1, HL^C\_1, HH^C\_1} \\end{aligned}

(9)


Then, feature extraction leverages the second-level approximation coefficients LL^C\_2. Intra-channel features capture individual channel characteristics:

\\begin{aligned} \\mathcal {F}\_{\\text {intra}} = \\left\\{ LL^C\_2 \\in \\mathbb {R}^{\\frac{H}{4} \\times \\frac{W}{4}} \\mid C \\in \\{R, G, B\\} \\right\\} \\end{aligned}

(10)


inter-channel features are constructed through absolute spectral differences:

\\begin{aligned} \\mathcal {F}\_{\\text {inter}}&= \\Big \\{ LL^{C\_i C\_j}\_2 = \\left\| LL^{C\_i}\_2 - LL^{C\_j}\_2 \\right\| \\nonumber \\\&\\quad \\mid (C\_i, C\_j) \\in \\{(R,G), (R,B), (G,B)\\} \\Big \\} \\end{aligned}

(11)


the complete feature representation combines both aspects:

\\begin{aligned} \\mathcal {F}\_{\\text {total}} = \\mathcal {F}\_{\\text {intra}} \\cup \\mathcal {F}\_{\\text {inter}} \\end{aligned}

(12)


Following this, similarity assessment employs the Pearson Correlation Coefficient to quantify morphological discrepancies between sample (S\_a) and test (T\_e) patterns. For feature component _F_, the similarity metric is:

\\begin{aligned} \\rho \_F = \\frac{\\mathbb {E}\[(S\_a - \\mu \_{S\_a})(T\_e - \\mu \_{T\_e})\]}{\\sigma \_{S\_a} \\sigma \_{T\_e}} \\end{aligned}

(13)


where \\mathbb {E}\[\\cdot \] denotes the mathematical expectation, and \\mu  and \\sigma  are the mean and standard deviation, respectively.

Finally, the authentication score combines weighted intra-channel and inter-channel similarities:

\\begin{aligned} \\rho \_{\\text {final}} = \\sum \_{C \\in {R,G,B}} w\_C \\rho \_C + \\sum \_{C\_1 \\ne C\_2 \\in {R,G,B}} w\_{C\_1C\_2} \\rho \_{C\_1C\_2} \\end{aligned}

(14)


here, \\sum w\_C + \\sum w\_{C\_1C\_2} = 1 ensuring normalized weighting. This comprehensive approach effectively captures both channel-specific characteristics and cross-channel spectral relationships for robust anti-counterfeiting authentication.

The extraction process of Fourier-domain feature

#### 4.2.2 Fourier-domain feature

While wavelet-domain facilitates multi-resolution analysis with noise resilience and computational efficiency, its iterative decomposition and reconstruction processes may lead to potential feature degradation. Therefore, an adaptive weighted multi-resolution local phase quantization algorithm is proposed for color codes. The basic idea behind the approach is to construct a texture descriptor based on a binary encoding of quantized Fourier phases computed locally around each pixel. This approach ensures fuzzy invariance of the phase spectrum. The Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT) is used to divide the signal into short time segments, and then the Fourier transform is applied to each segment to obtain the analyzed results in a time-frequency two-dimensional plane. Phase information is then extracted from the STFT results statistically. The approach is insensitive to illumination variations and image blur, but may introduce noise. The STFT spectral feature extraction process is illustrated in Fig. [7](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#Fig7). The feature extraction framework comprises six sequential processing steps:

Step 1: Chromatic decomposition and differential feature generation

The input image is decomposed into its RGB spectral components, yielding the intra-channel feature set:

\\begin{aligned} \\mathcal {F}\_{\\text {intra}} = \\left\\{ E^C \\in \\mathbb {R}^{H \\times W} \\mid C \\in \\{R, G, B\\} \\right\\} \\end{aligned}

(15)


inter-channel differential features are generated through absolute spectral differences:

\\begin{aligned} \\mathcal {F}\_{\\text {inter}}&= \\Big \\{ E^{C\_i C\_j} = \\left\| E^{C\_i} - E^{C\_j} \\right\| \\nonumber \\\&\\quad \\mid (C\_i, C\_j) \\in \\{(R,G), (R,B), (G,B)\\} \\Big \\} \\end{aligned}

(16)


Step 2: Multi-scale spatial partitioning

The six feature components are partitioned into K \\times K non-overlapping sub-regions \\mathcal {L} = \\{L\_1, L\_2, \\dots , L\_{K^2}\\}, each with spatial dimensions (W/K) \\times (W/K) pixels.

Step 3: Multi-scale LPQ feature extraction

For each sub-region L\_i \\in \\mathcal {L}, LPQ features are extracted at multiple scales \\mathcal {S} = \\{s\_1, s\_2, \\dots , s\_m\\}. The information entropy of each LPQ histogram is computed:

\\begin{aligned} \\mathcal {H}\_i = \\left\\{ H\\left( \\text {LPQ}(L\_i, s\_j)\\right) \\mid s\_j \\in \\mathcal {S}\\right\\} \\end{aligned}

(17)


where H(\\cdot ) denotes the entropy function. The LPQ feature extraction process involves:

(a) Short-time fourier transform (STFT)

For each pixel _x_ in image _f_( _x_), the local frequency domain is obtained using STFT over a neighborhood \\mathcal {N}\_x:

\\begin{aligned} F(u, x) = \\sum \_{y \\in \\mathcal {N}\_x} f(x - y) e^{-q2\\pi u^T y} \\end{aligned}

(18)


where _x_ denotes the spatial coordinates, _y_ represents the relative position coordinates within the neighborhood, _u_ is the frequency vector, \\mathcal {N}{x} is the local neighborhood centered, e^{-q2\\pi u^T y} is the complex sinusoidal basis function, and _q_ denotes the imaginary unit.

(b) Frequency component extraction

\\begin{aligned} \\textbf{F}\_x = \\left\[ \\Re \\{F(\\textbf{u}\_k, x)\\},\ \\Im \\{F(\\textbf{u}\_k, x)\\} \\right\] \_{k=1}^{4} \\end{aligned}

(19)


where \\Re  and \\Im  denote the real and imaginary parts respectively, and \\textbf{u}\_k represents the _k_-th frequency orientation vector with \\textbf{u}\_1 = \[a, 0\]^T, \\textbf{u}\_2 = \[0, a\]^T, \\textbf{u}\_3 = \[a, a\]^T, \\textbf{u}\_4 = \[a, -a\]^T, where a = M^{-1}.

(c) Binary encoding

The LPQ value for each pixel is computed using the step function \\mathcal {H}(\\cdot ):

\\begin{aligned} f\_{\\text {LPQ}}(x) = \\sum \_{j=p}^{8} \\mathcal {H}(F\_x^p) \\cdot 2^{p-1} \\end{aligned}

(20)


where F\_x^p denotes the _p_-th component of \\textbf{F}\_x.

(d) Histogram construction

A 256-dimensional feature histogram is constructed from the LPQ values to represent phase information with blur invariance.

Step 4: Weighted multi-resolution feature fusion

The multi-scale features are fused using weighted combination:

\\begin{aligned} \\mathcal {F}\_{\\text {MR}} = \\left\\{ w\_i \\cdot \\mathcal {H}\_i \\mid i = 1, 2, \\dots , K^2\\right\\} \\end{aligned}

(21)


with \\sum \_{i=1}^{K^2} w\_i = 1 ensuring normalized weighting.

Step 5: Multi-channel feature integration

Features from all chromatic components are integrated:

\\begin{aligned} \\mathcal {F}\_{\\text {final}} = \\left\\{ \\mathcal {F}\_{\\text {MR}}^R,\ \\mathcal {F}\_{\\text {MR}}^G,\ \\mathcal {F}\_{\\text {MR}}^B,\ \\mathcal {F}\_{\\text {MR}}^{RG},\ \\mathcal {F}\_{\\text {MR}}^{RB},\ \\mathcal {F}\_{\\text {MR}}^{GB}\\right\\} \\end{aligned}

(22)


Step 6: Classification via chi-square distance metric

The chi-square distance metric is employed for classification:

\\begin{aligned} \\chi ^2(P, Q) = \\sum \_{k=1}^{D} \\frac{(P\_k - Q\_k)^2}{P\_k + Q\_k} \\end{aligned}

(23)


where P = \\{P\_k\\}\_{k=1}^D and Q = \\{Q\_k\\}\_{k=1}^D represent the normalized feature histograms extracted from reference and query samples, respectively, _D_ denotes the feature dimensionality.

## 5 Experiments

### 5.1 Datasets

#### 5.1.1 Standard dataset

The QR code version determines matrix density through module count, with higher versions enhancing texture fidelity by reducing block size. This improvement strengthens anti-counterfeiting capabilities but requires precise printing and scanning systems to mitigate noise and blur risks. Excessively small blocks, however, may exceed the reader’s resolution limits and cause recognition errors. Balancing counterfeit prevention and readability therefore remains critical. Figure [8](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#Fig8) shows three versions and two block-type QR codes, all in grayscale (0–255). The physical dimensions of security codes involve a tradeoff: larger sizes improve readability by revealing clearer details but also increase counterfeiting risk due to exposed patterns, whereas smaller sizes obscure key features and reduce robustness against noise or blurring. During standardization, resizing operations (cropping or adjustment) significantly influence anti-counterfeiting performance by defining block dimensions and frequency decomposition methods—for example, wavelet-domain analysis reduces resolution to 25%, while Fourier-domain partitioning operates directly on full-resolution components. Considering practical constraints, this study adopts Version 3 with a 16\\times 16 block scheme.

This study conducts quantitative experiments to identify the optimal grayscale range for anti-counterfeiting QR codes. A dataset of 10 base codes is generated, each rendered under 12 grayscale configurations ranging from \[0,0\] to \[150,255\], yielding 120 digital samples. Each sample is first printed and scanned to obtain a high-fidelity reference image. The same designs are then reprinted using consumer devices and captured via three smartphone models to simulate real-world counterfeiting. Evaluation is performed in two settings: (1) reference-based assessment uses scanned references to compute PSNR, SSIM, CORR, and NC (see Table [2](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#Tab2)), quantifying structural distortions; (2) no-reference assessment employs image entropy and PIQE (see Fig. [9](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#Fig9)) to evaluate texture complexity and perceptual degradation, respectively. Results show that the grayscale range \[0,175\] achieves the highest discriminability between genuine and counterfeit samples. Under this setting, the SSIM difference reaches 0.2025, entropy differs by 0.266, and PIQE scores increase from 34.23 (original) to 66.29 (forged). These findings demonstrate that the range \[0,175\] preserves authentic image quality while maximally exposing artifacts in counterfeit copies.

The evaluation considers two counterfeiting methods—scanning-printing and reprinting—and two acquisition devi-ces: a high-precision scanner and three smartphone models. Ten original anti-counterfeiting patterns are printed using an official printer (Fuji Xerox Apeosport-V C3375) and scanned as reference templates (Epson Perfection V39). Forged versions are generated by reprocessing these prints through 13 consumer-grade laser and inkjet printers (including the official model), yielding 130 counterfeit samples. Additionally, all original and forged patterns are photographed using three smartphone models, resulting in a total dataset of 810 images (30 originals+780 fakes).

#### 5.1.2 Dataset with interference

This paper establishes a test dataset of multi-class interference with genuine codes to test the generalization ability of the proposed algorithm. (1) Smartphone-based artificial interference dataset: The 30 codes taken by smartphones are interfered with different categories and different degrees with the following parameters: salt & pepper noise, brown noise, gaussian noise, speckle noise, defocus blur, motion blur, illumination, and scratch. (2) Smartphone-based actual interference dataset: It conducts systematic experiments in real-world scenarios, focusing on two typical interferences: illumination and blur. The illumination dataset is gathered across multiple time periods. It comprehensively captures the realistic imaging characteristics under natural lighting conditions, with a total of 100 images acquired. The blur dataset is established by deliberately introducing camera shake and incomplete focusing during the shooting process, thus directly capturing the blurred images generated in actual photography. This dataset also contains 100 images. It should be emphasized that both datasets are entirely derived from real shooting scenarios, without any artificial simulation or processing. Figures [10](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#Fig10) and [11](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#Fig11) show the effects of various interference on the CACQR code.

The smartphone-based artificial interference dataset encompasses eight degradation models, which are formally defined as follows:

1. (1)
Salt & pepper noise replaces pixels with extreme values:



\\begin{aligned} C\_{\\mathrm {Salt~ \\& ~pepper}}(x,y) = {\\left\\{ \\begin{array}{ll} 0, & \\text {with probability } p\_p \\\ L\_{\\max }, & \\text {with probability } p\_s \\\ C(x,y), & \\text {with probability } 1 - p\_s - p\_p \\end{array}\\right. } \\end{aligned}




(24)




    where p\_s and p\_p denote salt and pepper occurrence probabilities, respectively.

2. (2)
Brown noise introduces spatially correlated variations through position-dependent variance:



\\begin{aligned} C\_{\\text {brown}}(x,y)&= C(x,y) + \\eta \_B(x,y), \\quad \\eta \_B(x,y) \\sim \\mathcal {N}\\left( 0, \\sigma \_B^2(x,y)\\right) \\end{aligned}




(25)






\\begin{aligned} \\sigma \_B^2(x,y)&= \\alpha \_B \\cdot U(x,y), \\quad U(x,y) \\sim \\mathcal {U}(0,1) \\end{aligned}




(26)




    where \\alpha \_B controls the maximum noise intensity and _U_( _x_, _y_) generates spatial variance patterns.

3. (3)
Gaussian noise follows the classical additive white noise model:



\\begin{aligned} C\_{\\text {gaussian}}(x,y) = C(x,y) + \\eta \_G(x,y), \\quad \\eta \_G(x,y) \\sim \\mathcal {N}(\\mu \_G, \\sigma \_G^2) \\end{aligned}




(27)




    characterized by mean \\mu \_G and variance \\sigma \_G^2.

4. (4)
Speckle noise exhibits multiplicative characteristics:



\\begin{aligned} C\_{\\text {speckle}}(x,y) = C(x,y) \\cdot \\left\[ 1 + \\eta \_S(x,y)\\right\] , \\quad \\eta \_S(x,y) \\sim \\mathcal {N}(0, \\sigma \_S^2) \\end{aligned}




(28)




    with variance \\sigma \_S^2 controlling the multiplicative disturbance intensity.

5. (5)
Defocus blur arises from improper lens focus during image capture. Its degradation kernel is modeled as:



\\begin{aligned} C\_{defocus}(x,y) = {\\left\\{ \\begin{array}{ll} \\pi ^{-1}R^{-2}, & x^2 + y^2 \\le R^2 \\\ 0, & \\text {otherwise} \\end{array}\\right. } \\end{aligned}




(29)




    where _R_ is the blur radius.

6. (6)
Motion blur stems from camera-target displacement during exposure. Assuming uniform linear motion, the kernel becomes:



\\begin{aligned} C\_{motion}(x,y) = {\\left\\{ \\begin{array}{ll} L^{-1}, & \\sqrt{x^2+y^2} \\le L/2 \ \\& \ y/x = \\tan \\theta \\\ 0, & \\text {otherwise} \\end{array}\\right. } \\end{aligned}




(30)




    where _L_ is motion length and \\theta  the motion direction angle.

7. (7)
Illumination effect caused by a strong, non-uniform light source is mathematically modeled as follows:



\\begin{aligned} C\_{\\text {illumination}}(x,y) = C(x,y) \\left\[ 1 + \\alpha \_I \\cdot \\exp \\left( -\\frac{(x - x\_c)^2 + (y - y\_c)^2}{2\\sigma ^2}\\right) \\right\] , \\end{aligned}




(31)




    where \\alpha \_I  is the peak intensity gain at the center of the light spot, (x\_c, y\_c) denotes the spot center coordinates, and \\sigma  controls the spatial extent of the illumination.

8. (8)
Scratch artifact is modeled as a superposition of dark line segments with specified length, width, and orientation. The degradation process can be formulated as:



\\begin{aligned} C\_{\\text {scratch}}(x,y) = C(x,y) \\cdot \\Bigg \[1 - \\beta \\cdot \\sum \_{k=1}^{N} S\_k(x,y; L\_k, w\_k, \\theta \_k)\\Bigg \], \\end{aligned}




(32)




    where _N_ is the number of scratches, \\beta \\in (0,1\] controls the darkening intensity, and S\_k(x,y;L\_k,w\_k,\\theta \_k) denotes the spatial mask function of the _k_-th scratch, parameterized by its length L\_k, width w\_k, and orientation angle \\theta \_k.

All the experiments are performed using MATLAB R2021a on a PC with an Intel Core i7-7567 CPU and 8GB RAM. In this paper, Precision, Recall, Accuracy and F1-score are used as the metrics to measure the anti-counterfeiting performance of various methods. The metrics are formulated as follows:

\\begin{aligned} Precision= & \\frac{{Gg}}{{Gg + Fg}} \\end{aligned}

(33)


\\begin{aligned} Recall= & \\frac{{Gg}}{{Gg + Gf}}\\end{aligned}

(34)


\\begin{aligned} Accuracy= & \\frac{{G\\mathrm{{g}} + Ff}}{{Gg + Fg + Ff + Gf}}\\end{aligned}

(35)


\\begin{aligned} F1-score= & \\frac{2 \\times Precision \\times Recall}{Precision + Recall} \\end{aligned}

(36)


here, _Gg_ is the number of genuine patterns correctly identified as genuine, _Fg_ is the number of forged patterns incorrectly identified as genuine, _Gf_ is the number of genuine patterns incorrectly identified as forged, and _Ff_ is the number of forged patterns correctly identified as forged.

### 5.2 Results

#### 5.2.1 Standardized dataset

Figure [12](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#Fig12) shows the recognition effect of the Wavelet-domain and Fourier-domain features proposed in this paper on the standardized dataset. The horizontal coordinate represents the sequential number of the code, with each sequence containing 3 genuine codes and 39 counterfeiting codes. The Wavelet-domain feature on the ordinate indicates the similarity between the code under test and the template, with higher values indicating closer resemblance. The Fourier-domain feature on the ordinate signifies the chi-square distance between the code under test and the template, with lower values suggesting a closer match. It can be seen that two algorithms perform better on genuine and scanning-printing dataset, with an accuracy of 100 \\%. However, in the reprinting dataset, both kinds of algorithms have a certain degree of overlap problem, but through the cascading combination of the two kinds of algorithms, this problem can be effectively solved to ensure the classification accuracy. In addition, the selection of different threshold values has a significant impact on the classification, and the specific values can be obtained through experiments. Figure [13](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#Fig13) shows the ROC curves for Wavelet-domain and Fourier-domain features, respectively, under two falsifications. The ROC curve is a graphical tool used to assess classifier performance by illustrating the relationship between true positive rate (TPR) and false positive rate (FPR) at different classification thresholds. The TPR indicates the proportion of positive samples that are correctly categorized as positive samples, whereas the FPR indicates the proportion of negative samples misclassified as positive. By adjusting the classification thresholds, one can obtain different values of TPR and FPR to plot the ROC curve. The AUC (area under the curve) is a metric that can be used to judge the performance of the classifier. The AUC value typically ranges from 0.5 to 1, where 0.5 indicates that the classifier’s performance is equivalent to random guessing, and 1 indicates that the classifier performs perfectly. Therefore, a larger AUC value indicates better classifier performance. The AUC values for scanning-printing forgery and reprinting forgery are both close to 1, indicating strong performance in identifying the type of forgery. Using these two features together can lead to even better identification results.

To evaluate the proposed algorithm’s effectiveness in detecting scan-printed forgeries, a systematic comparison is conducted against several state-of-the-art methods. Experimental images are divided into 4\*4 sub-blocks, forming a dataset containing 160 sample patterns, 480 genuine patterns, 6240 scan-printed counterfeit patterns, and 6240 duplicate-printed counterfeit patterns. For the machine learning texture-based methods (RALBGC (Khadiri et al. [2018](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#ref-CR10 "Khadiri I, Kas M, Merabet Y et al (2018) Repulsive-and attractive local binary gradient contours: new and efficient feature descriptors for texture classification. Inf Sci 467:634–653")), RAMBP (Alkhatib and Hafiane [2019](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#ref-CR1 "Alkhatib M, Hafiane A (2019) Robust adaptive median binary pattern for noisy texture classification and retrieval. IEEE Trans Image Process 28(11):5407–5418")), and LGONBP (Song et al. [2021](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#ref-CR13 "Song T, Feng J, Luo L et al (2021) Robust texture description using local grouped order pattern and non-local binary pattern. IEEE Trans Circ Syst Video Technol 31(1):189–202"))) and deep learning methods (ResNet18 (He et al. ( [2016](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#ref-CR9 "He K, Zhang X, Ren S et al (2016) Deep residual learning for image recognition. In: 2016 IEEE conference on computer vision and pattern recognition. pp 770–778"))) and FG-DPANet (You et al. [2023](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#ref-CR23 "You C, Zheng H, Guo Z et al (2023) Identification of a printed anti-counterfeiting code based on feature guidance double pool attention networks. Comput Mater Contin 75(2):3431–3452"))), 50% of genuine and counterfeit patterns are used for training and the remaining 50% for testing; results are averaged over ten independent runs. Template-matching methods (DFDA (Wang et al. [2023](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#ref-CR17 "Wang T, Zheng H, You C et al (2023) A texture-hidden anti-counterfeiting QR code and authentication method. Sensors 23(2):795")), TACA (Wang et al. [2024](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#ref-CR16 "Wang T, Zheng H, Guo Z et al (2024) Anti-counterfeiting textured pattern. Vis Comput 40(3):2139–2160")), CLTP (Zheng et al. [2022](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#ref-CR24 "Zheng Z, Xu B, Ju J et al (2022) Circumferential local ternary pattern: new and efficient feature descriptors for anti-counterfeiting pattern identification. IEEE Trans Inf Forensics Secur 17:970–981"))) are tested directly without a training phase. Performance metrics (Precision, Recall, Accuracy, F1-score) on the scanning-printing forgery dataset are summarized in Fig. [14](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#Fig14). The proposed method, together with LGONBP and ResNet18, achieves perfect scores (100%) across all four metrics, demonstrating strong anti-counterfeiting capability. Statistical significance of F1-score differences is further assessed using Welch’s one-tailed t-test with ten independent replications (see Table [3](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#Tab3)). The proposed method shows no statistically significant difference in F1-score compared to LGONBP (p=0.500) or ResNet18 (p=0.500), confirming comparable top-tier performance. In contrast, it significantly outperforms all other methods (p<0.0001). DFDA and TACA attain 100% accuracy but lower recall (93.33%), yielding a lower F1-score (0.965). RALBGC achieves high recall (93.33%) yet lower precision (87.5%), resulting in an F1-score of 0.903 (Cohen’s d=9.58). CLTP obtains moderate scores (F1-score=0.847) and the lowest accuracy (30.12%), suggesting a potential model-dataset mismatch. FG-DPANet delivers solid performance but its F1-score (0.889) remains significantly lower (Cohen’s d=10.58). RAMBP performs weakest, with only 13.33% recall and an F1-score of 0.211, indicating its inadequacy for this task. In summary, the proposed algorithm achieves cutting-edge performance on scanning-printing forgery detection, matching the best existing methods (LGONBP, ResNet18) while statistically surpassing all other compared approaches. The analysis also highlights that in class-imbalanced datasets, relying solely on accuracy can be misleading (e.g., RAMBP, CLTP); the F1-score, complemented by rigorous statistical testing, offers a more reliable and comprehensive evaluation of model performance.

In the task of reprinting forgery detection, the proposed algorithm achieves perfect performance across all evaluated metrics, including precision, recall, accuracy, and F1-score (see Fig. [15](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#Fig15)). In contrast, ResNet18 also demonstrates strong classification capability, with high precision (93.54%), recall (96.66%), and an F1-score of 95.07, indicating its robust feature extraction ability. To rigorously evaluate performance differences, statistical significance tests were conducted on the F1-score results. As shown Table [4](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#Tab4), the proposed method significantly outperforms all comparison methods (p<0.0001 in all cases), including a notable advantage over ResNet18 (mean F1-score difference=0.0492, Cohen’s d=4.24). This confirms the proposed method’s statistically superior performance even when compared to high-performing deep-learning baselines. Among the other methods, DFDA, TACA, FG-DPANet, and LGONBP share a common profile of high recall (\\ge 86.66%) but relatively lower precision (68.42%–82.35%), which leads to their reduced F1-scores (76.47–87.50). This pattern suggests that these methods are generally effective at identifying genuine samples but are more prone to false positives in the reprinting scenario. RALBGC maintains a balanced trade-off between precision and recall (both 86.66%), resulting in an F1-score of 86.66 and high accuracy (98.09%), indicating stable overall classification performance. Two methods exhibit particularly notable limitations. RAMBP shows extremely low recall (13.33%) and precision (22.22%), with a correspondingly low F1-score (16.66). Its relatively high accuracy (90.47%) can be attributed to the dataset’s class imbalance, where predicting “counterfeit” for most samples yields high accuracy but fails to capture genuine patterns effectively. CLTP achieves moderate precision (78.13%) and recall (83.33%) but the lowest accuracy among all methods (97.14%), which, combined with its significantly lower F1-score (80.65), may indicate issues such as overfitting or a mismatch between the model’s design and the texture characteristics of reprinting forgeries. In summary, the proposed method not only achieves optimal performance across all standard metrics but also demonstrates statistically significant superiority over existing approaches, including the well-established ResNet18. The analysis underscores the importance of evaluating multiple metrics alongside statistical testing, particularly in imbalanced forgery-detection tasks where accuracy alone can be misleading. These results provide a comprehensive benchmark for future work in anti-counterfeiting QR code analysis.

#### 5.2.2 Dataset with interference

This study conducts a series of controlled experiments to systematically evaluate the robustness of the proposed method and benchmark algorithms under eight types of image degradations. All experiments follow a parameterized design, with detailed parameter settings listed in Table [5](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#Tab5). Each degradation type is tested at four incrementally increasing intensity levels to comprehensively examine how performance evolves as interference strengthens.

Salt & pepper noise (see Fig. [16](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#Fig16)) is simulated by replacing pixel values with extreme values at random positions with probability p\_{\\text {salt}} = p\_{\\text {pepper}} \\in \\{0.005, 0.01, 0.015, 0.02\\}. The proposed method maintains 100% recall across all noise levels. Among the comparison methods, RALBGC completely fails (0% recall), and RAMBP declines from 13.33% to 3.33%, indicating that traditional texture-based methods are highly sensitive to impulse noise. LGONBP performs well at low noise density (96.66% recall at p = 0.005) but gradually decreases to 80% as the noise probability rises. DFDA achieves 93.33% recall under low noise but drops to 0% at p = 0.02, revealing its lack of robustness against dense impulse noise. TACA and CLTP exhibit relatively stable performance, decreasing from 93.33% to 80% and fluctuating between 66.66% and 73.33%, respectively. Brown noise (see Fig. [17](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#Fig17)) is simulated through spatially correlated intensity fluctuations, whose variance is controlled by the scaling factor \\alpha \_B \\in \\{0.01, 0.02, 0.03, 0.04\\}. The proposed method maintains 100% recall, confirming effective suppression of low-frequency drift. In contrast, RALBGC and RAMBP remain completely ineffective; LGONBP shows a non-monotonic response (rising from 50% to 100%), reflecting algorithmic instability; DFDA decreases linearly from 76.67% to 0%; TACA and CLTP exhibit gradual performance decay, declining from 90% to 76.66% and from 86.66% to 73.33%, respectively. Gaussian noise (see Fig. [18](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#Fig18)) follows a zero-mean additive model with variance \\sigma \_G^2 \\in \\{0.025, 0.05, 0.075, 0.10\\}. The proposed method still achieves 100% recall. Among the benchmarks, RALBGC and RAMBP are completely ineffective; LGONBP maintains high performance (96.66\\% \\rightarrow 93.33\\%); DFDA drops from 83.33% to 50%; TACA remains stable around 93.33%; CLTP shows little variation (86.66\\% \\rightarrow 86.66\\%); while FG-DPANet deteriorates markedly from 76.66% to 36.66%. Speckle noise (see Fig. [19](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#Fig19)), a multiplicative degradation, is applied with variance \\sigma \_S^2 \\in \\{0.05, 0.10, 0.15, 0.20\\}. This type of noise poses a severe challenge to most methods. The proposed method preserves 100% recall throughout. In comparison, LGONBP reaches 100% at \\sigma \_S^2 = 0.1 but plunges to 0% at \\sigma \_S^2 = 0.4, indicating high instability; DFDA fails completely after \\sigma \_S^2 = 0.1; TACA decreases gradually from 93.33% to 70%; CLTP declines from 80% to 50%; and deep-learning-based methods fail entirely at higher variances. These results highlight the unique advantage of the proposed method under multiplicative noise.

Defocus blur (see Fig. [20](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#Fig20)) is simulated using a circular averaging filter with radii R \\in \\{2, 3, 4, 5\\} pixels. The proposed method retains 100% recall. Motion blur (see Fig. [21](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#Fig21)) is generated with a linear motion kernel of lengths L \\in \\{4, 6, 8, 10\\} pixels. The proposed method again maintains 100% recall. Comparison methods exhibit the following trends: RALBGC fails completely; RAMBP gives 23.33% recall at L = 4 and then fails; LGONBP achieves 66.66% at L = 4 and 30% at L = 6, after which it fails; DFDA decreases from 66.67% to 0%; TACA declines from 93.33% to 80%; CLTP remains above 86.66%; and FG-DPANet drops to 3.33% at L = 8. Illumination variation (see Fig. [22](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#Fig22)) is simulated by applying a global multiplicative factor \\alpha \_I \\in \\{0.075, 0.15, 0.225, 0.3\\} to elevate brightness. The proposed method maintains 100% recall except at the lowest brightness level (\\alpha \_I = 0.3), where recall slightly decreases to 90%. Among the benchmarks, RALBGC gives 6.66% recall at \\alpha \_I = 0.075 and then fails; RAMBP fluctuates irregularly (16.66\\% \\rightarrow 10\\%); LGONBP improves as brightness elevates (20\\% \\rightarrow 70\\%); DFDA oscillates around 50%; TACA fails completely after \\alpha \_I = 0.15; while CLTP delivers the best performance among the benchmarks, consistently achieving \\ge 96.66\\% recall. Scratch attacks (see Fig. [23](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#Fig23)) are simulated by adding N \\in \\{10, 20, 30, 40\\} randomly oriented dark line segments. The proposed method achieves 100% recall for N \\le 30 and 99% for N = 40. In contrast, RALBGC fails completely; RAMBP peaks at 23.33% for N = 20; LGONBP increases slowly from 3.33% to 16.66%; DFDA declines from 43.3% to 20%; TACA maintains 100% recall for N \\le 30 but drops at N = 40; and CLTP decreases from 50% to 33%.

To comprehensively evaluate the practicality of different methods under realistic imaging conditions, this paper systematically tested three key metrics—illumination robustness, blur resistance, and computational efficiency—on a unified experimental platform. The results are presented in Figs. [24](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#Fig24), [25](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#Fig25), and [26](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44443-025-00453-0#Fig26), respectively. Experimental data indicate that the proposed method achieves a perfect score (100%) in both illumination invariance and blur robustness, significantly outperforming existing methods. This advantage stems from its feature extraction mechanism, which integrates multi-scale gradient normalization and adaptive local thresholding. This mechanism effectively suppresses global brightness interference while preserving critical edge structures, thereby achieving dual invariance to both photometric and geometric degradation. Comparative methods exhibit distinct performance characteristics: RALBGC and LGONBP perform excellently under illumination variation (both reaching 100%), but their heavy reliance on clear edge representation leads to a sharp decline in performance under blur (dropping to 0% and 32%, respectively). TACA and CLTP show relatively good blur resistance (93% and 80%, respectively); however, because their feature extraction is sensitive to gradient stability, they respond inadequately in low-light or backlit scenes with weak textures, resulting in limited illumination robustness (83% and 62%, respectively). DFDA achieves 100% illumination stability and 51% blur robustness through its lightweight filter structure, but its template-matching mechanism offers limited recognition accuracy under complex degradations. Deep learning-based methods, ResNet18 and FG-DPANet, achieve overall performance scores of 75%/62% and 68%/54%, respectively; constrained by their training data distributions, their generalization ability remains insufficient for unseen real-world degradation patterns. In terms of computational efficiency, DFDA is the fastest (0.05s per image) due to its direct template matching, but this comes at the cost of recognition accuracy. CLTP requires the longest processing time (6.95s per image) because of its complex iterative matching mechanism. The proposed method maintains 100% recognition accuracy while achieving a processing time of 1.82s per image, demonstrating good real-time performance. Its efficiency surpasses that of most traditional methods and deep learning models, reflecting an excellent balance between accuracy and speed.

Comparison of runtime

Conventional machine learning texture-based and deep learning methods exhibit reduced robustness under noise and blur interference due to three inherent limitations. First, imaging distortions cause structural degradation, disrupting critical texture regularities such as spatial repetition, directional symmetry, and localized frequency patterns that these algorithms depend on for feature extraction. Second, pollution leads to grayscale homogenization in local regions, diminishing the contrast gradients necessary for discriminative texture analysis. Third, interference-induced data distribution shifts create training-testing domain gaps, where models struggle to generalize to degraded texture patterns not encountered during training. Our algorithm addresses these limitations through adaptive multi-scale feature fusion, achieving consistent 100% recall across various noise types and blur intensities. However, the classification performance could deteriorate in the face of stronger interference. Therefore, when determining the classification threshold, a balance needs to be struck between ensuring accuracy and increasing resistance to interference. This means that while it is possible to increase resistance to attacks by raising the threshold, doing so may sacrifice some of the accuracy. Conversely, lowering the threshold may increase accuracy but may reduce the robustness of the method in the face of attacks. In summary, the method should take into full consideration the trade-off between accuracy and attack resistance when choosing an appropriate classification threshold for attack scenarios of different intensities.

## 6 Conclusion

This paper introduces an anti-counterfeiting QR code scheme tailored for mobile terminal applications, along with its accompanying authentication algorithm. The proposed solution exhibits excellent performance in terms of practicality, forgery resistance, interference resistance, and reprinting resistance, demonstrating both academic significance and commercial viability. This approach is particularly suitable for high-stakes anti-counterfeiting applications that demand strict color consistency and texture authenticity, such as luxury packaging, critical documents, and brand protection. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method excels in resisting various disturbances, including noise, blur, illumination, and scratches, as well as preventing duplicate forgery. Nevertheless, the approach still has certain limitations: under extreme imaging conditions, such as strong illumination, severe scratches, or cluttered background interference, the stability of feature extraction and the generalization capability of the model may be compromised. In light of these challenges, future research will focus on the following directions: (1) assessing robustness across a wide range of physical scales and complex real-world environments, (2) designing single-sample verification protocols with enhanced reliability, and (3) integrating deep neural networks to improve feature discrimination capabilities, thereby moving beyond conventional statistical pattern recognition frameworks.
